slides.title: Use of SHA-3 in the Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2) and IPsec
Discussions and improvements on RFC 9370 ADDKE algorithm
PQ/T Hybrid PKI Authentication in IKEv2
Hybrid Post-Quantum and Traditional Authentication for IKEv2
PQ/T Hybrid Composite Key Exchange & Reliable Transport for IKEv2
MLS for IPsec Group Key  Management

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Awfully quiet in here...

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: Yes please do that

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: and this is what customization strings are for.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: @Sophie, mic?

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: Yes, I'm at the mic

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: customization strings is just a formalization of things like the info field in hmac.  We have done this for a long time, and now it is "standardized".

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: asking for a friend (me): why was seven chosen? Were there 3 bits available?

chat.author: Adam R
chat.text: I think the original idea was that you might want a lattice-based algorithm, a code-based algorithm, an isogeny-based algorithm, etc

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: Just in case we will see advances in the math for a completely new PQC KEMs

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Why not 4, or 8, though?   :')

chat.author: Tero Kivinen
chat.text: Seven sounded like a good number...

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: 7 feels so arbitrary

chat.author: Tero Kivinen
chat.text: It is...

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: The problem was that the up/per bound for possible KEMs must be set once, we cannot add more ADDKE later. So we chosen them with large spare margin

chat.author: Tero Kivinen
chat.text: And it means we can support 8 in total, i.e., initial and 7 additional.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: true, if initial fits in 1 MTU

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Transform type is an 8-bit field, with hardly any allocation in 15 years. So didn't want to allocate a lot all at once. And yes, 7 is arbitrary.

chat.author: Tero Kivinen
chat.text: The initial one is mandatory... it might be "throwaway" in the future if we ever get CRQC and we do not have any PQ safe algorithms that fit in the first exchange.

chat.author: Ben S
chat.text: ML-KEM-768 can just about fit in the IKE_SA_INIT if you really squeeze it in there ;)

chat.author: Tero Kivinen
chat.text: The first packet really needs to be less than 500 octets.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: can this issue just be addressed through validation of configs?

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: Yes

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: Initiator should not propose controversal proposals, and if it does, it is broken

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Tero: Guilin should get closer to the mic

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: He's pretty much inaudible in the room too.

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: And clearly there may be cases when even with a non-controversal proposals the responder cannot choose one - but that is that. If the responder does not support KEM that the initiator marked as "required", then the SA cannot be established.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Security by payment in swiss francs

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: for the people online that was not audible

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Meetecho could we possibly boost the audio of the mics in the room? It's pretty hard to hear.

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: I'll notify the AV team

chat.author: Tapio Sokura
chat.text: Isn't FrodoKEM being published also as an open std, outside ISO

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Tapio: by whom?

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: Not yet published: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-longa-cfrg-frodokem/

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: and not actually adopted by CFRG? right?

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: Not (yet)

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: IRTF tends to have document cycles even longer than IETF.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Re-standardizing ISO-compatible FrodoKEM doesn't seem like a job for CFRG, more of a job for a Crypto Engineering WG

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Which doesn't exist, so documents describing ChaCha20-Poly1305 and Edwards curves came from CFRG

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I feel CFRG chairs indicated they don't want to do that anymore

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Yes, neither was re-standardizing something that ISO had already done.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: This draft should require support for the downgrade protection extension, otherwise it seems broken from the start

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: Nick Sullivan has a proposal for handling algorithms like this: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-sullivan-crypto-publication/

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: and that is the minimum, Sophie is right and this is hard to do right

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: @daniel:  that plan relies on a draft being published first by CFRG...(or it used to)

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
"It describes a dedicated working group model for
   coordinating Standards Track deployment of CFRG mechanisms..."

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: In particular, if you do not hash the transcript into the session key (which afaik ipsec doesn't) you can forge signatures to both sides to make it look like everyone wanted RSA

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: should we write draft-x-ipsecme-just-hash-everything-into-SKEYSEED :thinking:

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: (if you hash in the transcript you don't necessarily fix that either, but without the hash it's definitely broken)

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: The transcript (the full thanscript if downgrade-prevention draft is negotiated) is signed and linked to the session keys (via prf(SK_pi, ID) as per SIGMA. It isn't stirred into the session keys, but is is still linked to them.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: Signing it doesn't do anything

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: As the attacker can forge signatures

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: prf(SK_pi, ID) only binds the session keys to the signature, not the rest of the transcript

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: It bust also break into session keys for that.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: Yeah but that isn't hard in an unauthenticated exchange

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: As said, even hashing in everything doesn't really fix this, you can just think of it as the attacker having two connections one with the client and one with the server

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: Yeah, if you have a broken trust anchor, it doesn't matter what magic you do with the key schedule and transcript.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: And pretending to be the other side that happens to only understand RSA for each

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: No need to break the trust anchor, the fact that pure RSA is a potential negotiation outcome is enough

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: As you can break any RSA leaf key

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: I believe that downgrade-prevention draft prevents this :-)

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: the downgrade-prevention draft helps because then you need to break two signatures instead of just one

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: But you can do that, assuming both signatures are RSA

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: but it doesn't solve broken-crypto-is-trusted-for-authentication (which is fundamentally hard-to-impossible to solve)

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: Yes. It relies that at least one signature is unbreakable.

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: My assumption was that if you have a non-broken (PQ) trust anchor, the whole chain is PQ, so there's no RSA leaf to break.  But if you have a RSA trust anchor there's nothing that can help you.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: We need to figure out better PKI strategies to signal that legacy crypto is for legacy users and stuff like that

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: @thom, there's a proposal in TLS which I haven't read which I'm told does something like this: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-tls-trust-anchor-ids/

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: From what I can tell, the downgrade protection draft applies to the kex algorithm, not the authentication

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: The ongoing talk is in CFRG

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: it applies to anything that's negotiated

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: Yep, TLS has the same problem

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: I think there's two things in PKI: The web PKI, and everything else. And everything else follows what the web PKI does - same algorithms. So it matters whether hybrid certificates are going to show up or not.  Either way, we should probably do what TLS do.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Daniel Van Geest I think trust anchor ids is more for signaling which trust anchors are supported to the other side which will help set up and handle these diverse PKIs but I don't think it's specifically addressing the "you shouldn't trust classic signatures for this service anymore" type of issue

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: ... I don't think HKDF is the right reference here

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: @Thom, Bob lectured me about it at the hackathon and one of the points he hammered at was that it could be used to sandbox peers who still need to use old algorithms while letting everyone else get on with PQ.

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: but of course I need to read the draft and understand it myself as well.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: No, that doesn't really work

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: Clients need to decline traditional signatures for TLS to be downgrade protected

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: You can do some trust on first use type stuff with hsts, but in general you cannot negotiate auth, only keclx

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: *kex

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: MLKEM-1024's public key / ciphertext is 1568 bytes*

chat.author: Guilin WANG
chat.text: Including ECDHE in IKE_SA_INIT, it wilk be 1+7=8

chat.author: Valery Smyslov
chat.text: The paper I will be talking about: https://github.com/smyslov/draft-wang-ipsecme-kem-auth-ikev2/blob/main/analysis/pqc_ikev2_auth_v28.pdf

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: So this is replacing the group management of G-IKEv2 with the one from MLS?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: (The IKEv2 drafts also describe PCS as "forward secrecy" but that's probably because the wording predates all of the work on/following analysis of Signal)

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: This is pretty much exactly what I did in my thesis, I used MLS to establish a group key that could be fed into TLS (See chapter 6 https://pure.royalholloway.ac.uk/ws/files/33074422/2018HoylandJGPhD.pdf )

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: where is IKEv2 group key agreement used?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I'd seen it existing but never really understood the use cases

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: In the past, the not so well defined term PFS was often quite often used to mean FS+PCS

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: @Thom - I don't know about G-IKEv2. GDOI had some use in telecom and industry. Not much on the open Internet.

chat.author: Jonathan Hammell
chat.text: A minimal implementation of G-IKEv2 targeting constrained devices was noted in the WG slides of IETF-99  (https://www.ietf.org/proceedings/99/slides/slides-99-ipsecme-minimal-g-ikev2-implementation-01.pdf)

chat.author: Guilin WANG
chat.text: Sorry, I was away from the mic during my presentatiobs sometimes.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: It's about as popular as multicast. GDOI was usually used for generating keys to encrypt multicast.

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: Pretty sure Daniel, Sean, and I been here before...
